text:
INTEGRATED GENOMIC AND GERMLINE TESTING REPORT

Patient: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
Date of Birth: [on file]
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: High-Grade Serous Carcinoma, Bilateral Ovaries and Fallopian Tubes, FIGO Stage IIIC
Ordering Clinician: Gynecologic Oncology
Specimen for Somatic Testing: Omental biopsy (formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded)
Specimen for Germline Testing: Peripheral blood (EDTA)
Referring Institution: [Institution on file]

---

PART I: GERMLINE GENETIC TESTING

Test Methodology:
Multi-gene hereditary cancer panel performed via next-generation sequencing with reflex deletion/duplication analysis (MLPA). Genes analyzed include BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, BRIP1, CHEK2, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM, PALB2, RAD51C, RAD51D, and additional hereditary cancer genes. Analytical sensitivity >99% for single nucleotide variants and small indels; >95% for large deletions/duplications.

GERMLINE RESULT: POSITIVE — PATHOGENIC VARIANT IDENTIFIED

Gene: BRCA1
Nucleotide Change: c.5266dupC
Protein Change: p.Gln1756ProfsTer74
Alternate Designation: 5382insC
Zygosity: Heterozygous
Clinical Classification: PATHOGENIC
ClinVar Accession: ClinVar ID 37523
Inheritance: Autosomal dominant

Interpretation:
The c.5266dupC (5382insC) variant in BRCA1 is one of the most well-characterized pathogenic variants in the BRCA1 gene. This single-nucleotide insertion creates a frameshift at codon 1756, introducing a premature stop codon 74 amino acids downstream (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74). This results in a truncated, non-functional BRCA1 protein with loss of the BRCT domain, which is essential for DNA damage response and homologous recombination repair. The variant has been documented as a founder mutation in populations of Eastern European Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry and has been independently documented in West African populations, including those of Ghanaian descent, consistent with this patient's background. This variant has been observed in thousands of individuals with breast and ovarian cancer and segregates with disease in multiple high-risk families. Functional studies demonstrate absent or markedly reduced BRCA1 protein activity. Classification: Pathogenic (Class 5) per ACMG/AMP criteria (PVS1, PS1, PS4, PP1).

Clinical Significance:
Germline pathogenic BRCA1 variants confer a markedly elevated lifetime risk of epithelial ovarian cancer (approximately 40–46% by age 70) and breast cancer (approximately 55–72% by age 70). They are also associated with modestly elevated risk of pancreatic cancer. This variant is directly relevant to the patient's diagnosis of high-grade serous carcinoma and has important implications for systemic treatment selection (specifically, PARP inhibitor eligibility) and for cascade testing of at-risk family members.

Secondary/Additional Findings:
- BRCA2: No pathogenic or likely pathogenic variant identified. No large rearrangements detected.
- Lynch Syndrome Genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2): No pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants identified. MSI status by tumor testing reported separately.
- PALB2, ATM, RAD51C, RAD51D, BRIP1, CHEK2: No pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants identified. One variant of uncertain significance (VUS) identified in ATM: c.7271T>G (p.Val2424Gly), clinical significance unknown; not considered actionable at this time.
- EPCAM: No large deletion involving EPCAM detected.

Family History Correlation:
Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with breast cancer at age 38 and maternal grandmother with ovarian cancer at age 55, a pattern highly consistent with autosomal dominant BRCA1 hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC) syndrome. Cascade genetic testing is strongly recommended for first-degree relatives including siblings and adult children.

---

PART II: SOMATIC TUMOR NEXT-GENERATION SEQUENCING

Platform: FoundationOne CDx (Foundation Medicine, Inc.)
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) omental biopsy
Specimen Adequacy: Adequate for analysis; tumor cellularity estimated at 65%
Genomic Coverage: >300 cancer-relevant genes; full exonic coverage with select intronic and promoter regions; RNA fusion panel included
Analytical Sensitivity: SNVs/indels ≥5% allele frequency; copy number alterations ≥3 copies; gene fusions detectable by RNA sequencing

SOMATIC ALTERATIONS DETECTED

1. TP53 p.R248W — SHORT VARIANT (MISSENSE MUTATION)
   VAF: 68%
   Classification: Pathogenic — Gain-of-Function
   The R248W hotspot missense mutation is among the most common TP53 alterations in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, occurring in >96% of HGSC tumors. R248W results in loss of wild-type tumor suppressor function and gain of oncogenic function, including promotion of cell migration, invasion, and resistance to apoptosis. The high VAF (68%) in the context of estimated 65% tumor cellularity indicates clonal, homozygous or near-homozygous TP53 alteration, consistent with the diffuse aberrant p53 overexpression seen on immunohistochemistry (p53 diffuse strong, consistent with gain-of-function pattern). This is a ubiquitous driver alteration in HGSC and consistent with the histologic diagnosis.

2. BRCA1 p.Gln1756ProfsTer74 (c.5266dupC) — SHORT VARIANT (FRAMESHIFT)
   VAF: 82%
   Classification: Pathogenic
   This is the same frameshift variant identified in the germline at heterozygous state. The somatic VAF of 82% significantly exceeds the expected ~50% for heterozygous germline retention, providing strong evidence for loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at the BRCA1 locus in the tumor. LOH at BRCA1 with retention of the pathogenic allele is the canonical mechanism of biallelic BRCA1 inactivation in HGSC and is required for full homologous recombination deficiency (HRD). This finding confirms complete somatic loss of BRCA1 function in this tumor and is the principal molecular driver of the high HRD score (see below).

3. RB1 — DEEP DELETION (HOMOZYGOUS LOSS)
   Copy Number: <0.5 (estimated homozygous deletion)
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic
   Homozygous deletion of RB1 represents complete biallelic loss of the retinoblastoma tumor suppressor, resulting in unchecked cell cycle progression through the G1/S checkpoint. RB1 loss is identified in approximately 20–30% of high-grade serous ovarian carcinomas and is associated with a more aggressive phenotype. RB1 deletion may also have implications for sensitivity to certain CDK4/6 inhibitor-based strategies, though this is investigational in ovarian cancer.

4. CCNE1 — AMPLIFICATION
   Copy Number: 6.2 (high-level amplification)
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic
   Cyclin E1 (CCNE1) amplification results in overexpression of cyclin E1 protein, leading to aberrant CDK2-mediated phosphorylation of RB1 and dysregulated S-phase entry. CCNE1 amplification is observed in approximately 20% of HGSC and is typically mutually exclusive with BRCA1/2 biallelic inactivation, as it represents an alternative mechanism of HRD and genomic instability. However, co-occurrence with BRCA1 LOH, as seen in this case, has been described and may indicate evolutionary selection for additional drivers of genomic instability. CCNE1 amplification has been associated with relative platinum resistance in some studies; its coexistence with high HRD score in this patient warrants close monitoring of platinum response.

5. NF1 p.R1362* — SHORT VARIANT (NONSENSE)
   VAF: 18%
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic (Subclonal)
   This nonsense mutation in NF1 (neurofibromin 1), a RAS-GAP tumor suppressor, is present at a subclonal VAF of 18%, suggesting it arose in a minority tumor subpopulation following the founding TP53 and BRCA1 alterations. NF1 loss results in constitutive RAS-MAPK pathway activation. Subclonal NF1 mutations are recognized in HGSC as secondary events and may contribute to intratumoral heterogeneity and adaptive resistance. Clinical significance in this setting is uncertain; no approved targeted therapies directly targeting NF1 loss are available for ovarian cancer.

6. PIK3CA p.E545K — SHORT VARIANT (MISSENSE)
   VAF: 12%
   Classification: Pathogenic (Subclonal)
   The E545K hotspot mutation in PIK3CA (PI3-kinase catalytic subunit alpha) is a well-characterized activating mutation in the helical domain, resulting in constitutive PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway activation. Present at a subclonal VAF of 12%, this appears to represent a minority clonal event. While PIK3CA mutations are actionable in certain cancer types (e.g., PIK3CA-mutant breast cancer with alpelisib), their clinical significance as subclonal events in HGSC is investigational. PI3K pathway activation may contribute to treatment resistance in a subpopulation of tumor cells.

7. NOTCH3 p.R1626C — SHORT VARIANT (MISSENSE)
   VAF: 9%
   Classification: Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS)
   This missense variant in NOTCH3 is present at a very low VAF (9%), consistent with a late, minor subclonal event. NOTCH3 alterations have been described in various cancers; however, the functional consequence of p.R1626C has not been definitively established. This variant is classified as a VUS and is not considered actionable at this time.

NO ALTERATIONS DETECTED IN:
- BRCA2 (somatic): No pathogenic somatic variant identified
- KRAS, NRAS, HRAS: No activating mutations (as expected; RAS mutations are uncommon in HGSC)
- ERBB2 (HER2): No amplification or activating mutation; copy number 2.0 (diploid)
- CDK12: No mutation identified (CDK12 alterations represent an alternative HRD mechanism; absent here)
- NTRK1/2/3 fusions: Not detected by RNA sequencing
- ALK, ROS1, RET fusions: Not detected
- AR: No amplification
- PTEN: No mutation or deletion (PTEN intact, though PIK3CA subclonal mutation may partially activate PI3K pathway independently)
- ARID1A, ARID1B: No pathogenic variants detected
- DICER1: No pathogenic variant detected

---

GENOMIC SIGNATURES AND BIOMARKERS

Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB): 4.2 mutations/megabase (LOW)
Interpretation: TMB-low tumors are less likely to respond to immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy. Per FDA approved indications, pembrolizumab TMB-H threshold is ≥10 mut/Mb (tissue-agnostic). This tumor does not meet that threshold.

Microsatellite Status (MSI): Microsatellite Stable (MSS)
Method: Fragment analysis of 5 established microsatellite loci + NGS-based assessment
Interpretation: MSS tumors are not expected to benefit from PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint blockade based on MSI status alone. Lynch syndrome genes were also not mutated in the germline.

Genomic Loss of Heterozygosity (gLOH): 31%
Threshold for gLOH-high: ≥16%
Interpretation: HIGH. gLOH of 31% is markedly elevated and is a component of the composite HRD score. High gLOH reflects widespread allelic imbalance throughout the genome, consistent with severe homologous recombination deficiency.

HRD Score (Myriad myChoice CDx-equivalent composite score): 68
Threshold for HRD-positive: ≥33 (composite of LOH, telomeric allelic imbalance [TAI], and large-scale state transitions [LST])
Interpretation: HRD POSITIVE. Score of 68 indicates a high degree of genomic scarring consistent with profound deficiency in homologous recombination DNA repair. This is driven principally by biallelic BRCA1 inactivation (germline pathogenic variant + somatic LOH). HRD-positive status, particularly in the context of BRCA1 biallelic loss, is highly predictive of sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy and PARP inhibitors.
Component subscores (estimated):
  - Genomic LOH (gLOH): 31% (high)
  - Telomeric Allelic Imbalance (TAI): elevated
  - Large-Scale State Transitions (LST): elevated

BRCA1 Promoter Methylation: ABSENT
Interpretation: No evidence of epigenetic silencing of BRCA1 via promoter hypermethylation. The BRCA1 deficiency in this tumor is accounted for entirely by the germline pathogenic frameshift variant with somatic LOH, not by epigenetic silencing.

---

SUMMARY OF ACTIONABLE FINDINGS

Tier I (Strong Clinical Significance — Directly Actionable):

1. Germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74) with somatic LOH at BRCA1 locus
   - This combination confirms complete biallelic BRCA1 inactivation in the tumor, the most predictive molecular biomarker of PARP inhibitor sensitivity in ovarian cancer.
   - FDA-approved PARP inhibitors with indications in germline BRCA1/2-mutated ovarian cancer:
     * Olaparib (Lynparza): FDA-approved as maintenance therapy following complete or partial response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy (SOLO-1 trial); also approved for relapsed germline BRCA-mutated ovarian cancer.
     * Niraparib (Zejula): FDA-approved as first-line maintenance following complete or partial response to platinum-based chemotherapy in HRD-positive advanced ovarian cancer (PRIMA trial); also approved in germline BRCA-mutated relapsed ovarian cancer.
     * Rucaparib (Rubraca): FDA-approved for germline/somatic BRCA-mutated advanced ovarian cancer following 2 or more prior platinum-based chemotherapies.
   - HRD-positive status (score 68) further supports PARP inhibitor benefit even beyond BRCA-mutant status.
   - Germline status also has therapeutic implications in the recurrent/platinum-resistant setting and for enrollment in PARP inhibitor-based combination trials.

2. HRD-Positive Status (gLOH 31%, HRD score 68)
   - Independent of BRCA1 status, HRD-positivity is an FDA-approved biomarker for niraparib maintenance in first-line ovarian cancer (PRIMA/ENGOT-OV26 trial, any HRD-positive subset).

Tier II (Potential Significance — Investigational or Uncertain Actionability):

3. CCNE1 amplification (copy number 6.2)
   - High-level CCNE1 amplification may predict relative resistance to platinum-based therapies in some studies; however, outcomes data in the setting of concurrent high HRD are limited. CDK2 inhibitors targeting CCNE1-amplified cancers are in early clinical investigation. No approved targeted therapy currently available.
   - Relevant for potential enrollment in CCNE1-amplified ovarian cancer clinical trials.

4. PIK3CA p.E545K (subclonal, VAF 12%)
   - Activating PIK3CA mutation present subclonally. PI3K pathway inhibition with alpelisib is approved in PIK3CA-mutated HR+/HER2- breast cancer; no approved indication in ovarian cancer. Relevant for potential clinical trial enrollment.

5. NF1 p.R1362* (subclonal, VAF 18%)
   - Subclonal NF1 loss may contribute to adaptive RAS pathway activation. MEK inhibitors are approved in NF1-associated neurofibromatosis but not in ovarian cancer; investigational context only.

6. RB1 homozygous deletion
   - Complete RB1 loss may have implications for CDK4/6 inhibitor resistance and could potentially influence sensitivity to alternative cell cycle-targeted agents. Investigational in HGSC.

Not Actionable at This Time:
- NOTCH3 p.R1626C (VUS, subclonal)
- ATM c.7271T>G (germline VUS, uncertain significance)

---

CLINICAL TRIAL ELIGIBILITY NOTE:
This patient may be eligible for clinical trials targeting:
- BRCA1/2-mutated and/or HRD-positive ovarian cancer (PARP inhibitor combinations, ATR inhibitors, WEE1 inhibitors)
- CCNE1-amplified ovarian cancer (CDK2 inhibitor trials)
- PI3K pathway-altered gynecologic cancers
Enrollment in genomics-driven clinical trials is encouraged.

---

GERMLINE COUNSELING NOTE:
Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant identified. Results carry significant implications for the proband and biological relatives. Genetic counseling visit is strongly recommended to review:
1. Cancer risk implications for the patient (including breast cancer surveillance with annual MRI and mammography; consideration of risk-reducing bilateral mastectomy)
2. Cascade testing recommendations for first-degree relatives (siblings, adult children, parents if living)
3. Reproductive implications
4. Psychosocial support resources

This report was generated using validated NGS methodology and reviewed by a board-certified molecular pathologist and clinical geneticist. Results should be interpreted in the context of the full clinical picture by a qualified healthcare provider.

Signing Pathologist: [Molecular Pathology, on file]
Genetic Counselor: [Hereditary Cancer Program, on file]
Report Status: FINAL
